symbols: towering figure above fallen forms — the weight of sin and its consequences cascading downward
tumbling contorted bodies — the spiritual disintegration of those who wander from the Father
ruined cityscape — the 'distant country' of waste and desolation where the prodigal squanders his inheritance
blue cold tones — spiritual death and the chill of separation from God
description: A surrealist painting in cool blue-grey tones showing an enormous monument-like form composed of intertwined human figures. A single figure sits enthroned at the top, while below, multiple bodies tumble and fall in contorted poses, merging with each other and with architectural elements. Classical buildings and domes of a ruined city surround the base. A circular pool or fountain sits at the very bottom. The scale is monumental, the human forms merging into something between sculpture and nightmare. The overall atmosphere is cold, desolate, and haunting.
theological_description: This surrealist allegory takes inspiration from the parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:11-32), extending it into a vision of collective spiritual exile. Jesus told of a son who 'set off for a distant country and there squandered his wealth in wild living' (Luke 15:13), eventually finding himself destitute and feeding pigs — the ultimate degradation for a Jewish man. The towering mass of intertwined, falling human forms visualizes the cascading consequences of turning away from the Father — each generation of prodigality dragging others downward. The ruined classical city suggests a civilization built on human pride now crumbling into decay, echoing the builders of Babel who said 'Come, let us build ourselves a city, with a tower that reaches to the heavens' (Genesis 11:4). Yet even in this bleak vision, the parable's promise endures: 'While he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms around him and kissed him' (Luke 15:20). The cold desolation depicted here is not the final word — it is the 'distant country' from which return is always possible.

scripture_refs.reason: The prodigal son squandering his inheritance in a distant country — the allegory's foundation
The tower of Babel — human pride building monuments that crumble, visible in the ruined cityscape
The father's enduring compassion — the hope that counterbalances the desolation depicted
